author: @Russmayra
text: I want to roll a wight down the slope of my property to get power then i need to pull it back up the hill with solar or wind. I will need a large ratchet.

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
what mechanical advantage would you be looking at needing to assist the "motor" in pulling the weight back up? worm drives have large value of  mechanical advantage (force out divided by force in) and could  in this application have an the advantage that a ratchet wouldn't be needed.
there is also the simpler mechanism of a differential windlass? nothing more than two winch drums of different diameters that are locked onto a common shaft. when shaft is turned rope unwinds from one drum, down to a hook the load is attached to, and then winds back on to the 2nd drum. because the drums are different diameters that means on turning there's a difference in the amount of rope played out/in. closer the diameters are, the greater the mechanical advantage  and again at large values of mechanical advantage won't need a ratchet

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
perhaps have a look into water powered inclined planes, funicular/cliff railways etc.
they had two carriages running on rails on straight slope. cable ran between them and around a large pulley at the top of the slope. what would drive the water powered ones was the difference in weight between the carriages. both carriages had a water tank underneigh. when carriages were at the extreme of that run... carriage at the bottom would release water from it's tank, and carriage at the top would have water added to it's tank. then once ready for movement, brakes would be released. historically a stream usually provided water for the carriage at top, and water from the bottom carriage would run to waste, but there's nothing other than such needing a power source to stop water being pumped back up.
NB it's  possible to avoid laying 4 rails all way up slow... there are arrangements which have passing loops in the middle of the slope, and allow use of 3 or even 2 rails for bulk of slope.

author: @Gucio37
text: Hey, I saw your previous video on the button generator and when I was mopping the floor later on I noticed the spin mop bucket e.g. Vileda easy-wring spin mop bucket (not sure how common they are in UK, but you can search YT for examples) - you press the pedal and the "rotor" spins and keep spinning, while the pedal comes back with a spring. You can get it to pretty good speeds  with couple of pushes. Might be something easy to turn into such generator as well, as it seems to have exactly the same idea as your button generator, probably using some kind of ratchet as well. might also be fun to take apart. Cheers Rob , always looking forward to the next video
